Challenge 7: The Sphere Packing Problem

For a dimension d∈ℕ, a unit packing of ℝd is a set C⊆ℝd of centres with ∥x−y∥≥2 for all distinct x,y∈C, so that the open unit balls around the centres are pairwise disjoint. Its upper density is

δ¯⁢(C)=lim supR→∞vol⁢(⋃x∈CB⁢(x,1)∩B⁢(0,R))vol⁢(B⁢(0,R)),

and the sphere-packing constant Δd=supCδ¯⁢(C) is the supremum over all unit packings; ΔdL denotes the supremum over packings whose centres form a lattice.

Open Problem 1.1 (The sphere-packing problem).

Determine the sphere-packing constant Δd for every dimension d. The value is known only for d∈{1,2,3,8,24}: the case d=3 is the Kepler conjecture [2], and d=8, d=24 were settled by Viazovska [3] and by Cohn, Kumar, Miller, Radchenko and Viazovska [1]. The largest solved case is d=24, and in each the optimum is attained by a lattice (ℤ, A2, the FCC lattice, E8, and the Leech lattice).

The universal version, posed as a prove-or-disprove challenge, asks whether lattices are always optimal: is Δd=ΔdL for every d? This holds in every solved dimension but is open in general and is widely expected to fail in high dimensions.

References

  • [1] H. Cohn, A. Kumar, S. D. Miller, D. Radchenko, and M. Viazovska (2017) The sphere packing problem in dimension 24. Annals of Mathematics 185 (3), pp. 1017–1033. doi: 10.4007/annals.2017.185.3.8.
  • [2] T. C. Hales (2005) A proof of the Kepler conjecture. Annals of Mathematics 162 (3), pp. 1065–1185. doi: 10.4007/annals.2005.162.1065.
  • [3] M. S. Viazovska (2017) The sphere packing problem in dimension 8. Annals of Mathematics 185 (3), pp. 991–1015. doi: 10.4007/annals.2017.185.3.7.
